repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
In favour of generating the call to the 'print' function directly through nodes.

My reasoning was:
* The utility code is not thread-safe in the freethreading build because it lazily caches the 'print' function and kwargs for a common argument combination.
* While looking at that, I decided it would benefit from vectorcall too.

I initially thought about rewriting it, but it seemed easier to use our existing vectorcall code generation and just do the transformation into Cython nodes.

I think the only likely performance degredation is earlier limited api (without vectorcall) using the "no newline" syntax. This will now generate a keyword dict each time rather than caching it.

comment_data.body: Isn't it rather the empty string?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -6816,61 +6816,40 @@ class PrintStatNode(StatNode):
     child_attrs = ["arg_tuple", "stream"]
 
     def analyse_expressions(self, env):
-        if self.stream:
-            stream = self.stream.analyse_expressions(env)
-            self.stream = stream.coerce_to_pyobject(env)
-        arg_tuple = self.arg_tuple.analyse_expressions(env)
-        self.arg_tuple = arg_tuple.coerce_to_pyobject(env)
-        env.use_utility_code(printing_utility_code)
-        if len(self.arg_tuple.args) == 1 and self.append_newline:
-            env.use_utility_code(printing_one_utility_code)
-        return self
-
-    nogil_check = Node.gil_error
-    gil_message = "Python print statement"
-
-    def generate_execution_code(self, code):
-        code.mark_pos(self.pos)
-        if self.stream:
-            self.stream.generate_evaluation_code(code)
-            stream_result = self.stream.py_result()
-        else:
-            stream_result = '0'
-        if len(self.arg_tuple.args) == 1 and self.append_newline:
-            arg = self.arg_tuple.args[0]
-            arg.generate_evaluation_code(code)
+        from .ExprNodes import (
+            DictNode, UnicodeNode, SimpleCallNode, GeneralCallNode,
+            NameNode
+        )
 
-            code.putln(
-                "if (__Pyx_PrintOne(%s, %s) < 0) %s" % (
-                    stream_result,
-                    arg.py_result(),
-                    code.error_goto(self.pos)))
-            arg.generate_disposal_code(code)
-            arg.free_temps(code)
+        func = NameNode(
+            self.pos,
+            name="print",
+            entry=env.builtin_scope().lookup_here("print")
+        )
+        if self.stream or not self.append_newline:
+            key_value_pairs = []
+            if self.stream:
+                key_value_pairs.append((
+                    UnicodeNode(self.pos, value=EncodedString("file")),
+                    self.stream
+                ))
+            if not self.append_newline:
+                key_value_pairs.append((
+                    UnicodeNode(self.pos, value=EncodedString("end")),
+                    UnicodeNode(self.pos, value=EncodedString(" "))
+                ))

comment_data.body: That matches what the old code did. I'm not sure I currently have a version of Python2 around to actually test the behaviour it's replicating. But it leaves a space so the next thing you print is separated.

comment_data.body:
> it leaves a space so the next thing you print is separated

… sort of. Py2 had something called "soft space" for which it didn't print the space just yet but remembered that there was a space yet to come and injected it when more was printed in the same line. I don't think that would be easy to emulate.

EDIT: See https://peps.python.org/pep-3105/#specification – there's intentionally no equivalent to it in Py3.

comment_data.body: … and since it was never available in Py3, the original C replacement that mapped `print` to `print()` also didn't have it. We're not breaking anything by replacing it with  Python implementation. Problem solved, keep walking, nothing to see here…

commit.message:
Get rid of specialized print utility code

In favour of generating the call to the 'print' function
directly through nodes.

My reasoning was:
* The utility code is not thread-safe in the freethreading build
  because it lazily caches the 'print' function and kwargs
  for a common argument combination.
* While looking at that, I decided it would benefit from vectorcall
  too.

I initially thought about rewriting it, but it seemed easier to
use out existing vectorcall code generation and just do the
transformation into Cython nodes.

I think the only likely performance degredation is earlier limited
api (without vectorcall) using the "no newline" syntax. This will
now generate a keyword dict each time rather than caching it.

title: Get rid of specialized print statement utility code
